title: Staying the course — Citrix CEO Mark Templeton expects to survive slump
author: Christine Winter (South Florida Sun-Sentinel, business writer)
abstract: South Florida Sun-Sentinel (May 2002) by Christine Winter on Citrix Systems Inc. (Fort Lauderdale, FL). After missing Q1 2002 sales/earnings forecasts, Citrix stock down ~50% since January 2002. Echoes spring 2000 when stock plunged 85% in 3 months. CEO Mark Templeton (back in charge after stepping down) says he isn't planning on going anywhere this time. Q1 2002 sales: $142.3M; adjusted net earnings 16¢/share. Stock closed up 8% (+$0.94 to $12.38) on news of new three-year Microsoft agreement (replacing 1997 agreement that paid Citrix $175M in royalties; new agreement gives continued Microsoft code access at no cost). 2001 sales: $591.6M; ~1,900 employees; ticker CTXS on Nasdaq. Quote from Peter Kastner, chief research officer for Aberdeen Group: 'Citrix has been tarred with the brush of a terrible economy, but we believe its strategy is sound. They are one of the few companies that can provide the kind of software that they do.' Counter-perspective from Dwight Davis, VP Summit Strategies (Kirkland WA): MetaFrame holding up well. Templeton blames IT spending environment, 'not poor execution, as before.' Citrix recently spent $55M on two new buildings to consolidate scattered worker sites. Templeton: 'We intend to be in a better position than ever to become that billion-dollar software company that we know we are.'
importance_rationale: Captures Kastner's chief-research-officer voice supporting Citrix's thin-client strategy in the 2001-2002 dot-com aftermath. Notable for Aberdeen's role in legitimizing Citrix during a dark stock-price period; Templeton's commitment to the billion-dollar target was eventually met.
relevance_rationale: Citrix existed as a public company until 2022 (acquired by Vista/Evergreen). Server-based application delivery (Citrix's core thesis) remains relevant in modern VDI, DaaS, and Zero-Trust Network Access markets. Microsoft RDS/AVD and VMware Horizon all descend from this lineage.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's strategic-soundness call was vindicated: Citrix's revenue exceeded $1B by 2006 (Templeton's stated goal); company peaked at >$3B revenue and $23B market cap before going private in 2022. The 'one of the few companies that can provide that kind of software' frame held — Citrix maintained thin-client/published-app leadership for two decades.
